t
Re
Fern PolrucAI- PRACTICES CouvtIssIoN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
March 26,2004
M. Delailah B. Adriatico
12745 Gorham Street
Moreno Valley, CA92553
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-04-053
Dear Ms. Adriatico:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the honorarium and
post-employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').I Since your request
asks for general guidance regarding potential application of the post-ernployment
provisions of the Act and does not identifu any payment you might receive as a result of a
future speaking engagement, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2
Our assistance is based on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not
act as a finder of fact when it provides informal assistance. Qn re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTTONS
1. Should you leave state service, would thepost-governmental employment
provisions of the Act restrict your activities in connection with the future publication and
promotion of a book you authored?
2. As a state employee, do the "honorarium" provisions of the Act prohibit you
from receiving payment in connection with the sale of your book, or seminars or
workshops you provide to train others in the methodologies described in your book?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The post-governmental ernployment provisions of the Act consist of two
prohibitions: first, a "one-year ban" prohibiting a former state anployee from
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
:


I
File No. I-04-053
Page No. 2
communicating with or appearing before his or her former agency to influence the
agency's administrative or legislative action; and second, a "permanent ban" barring a
state employee from "switching sides" in any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
on which the employee worked while in state service.3 Thus, should you leave state
service, the post-employment provisions of the Act would not apply to the sale and
promotion of your book, or the training you may provide to others in the methodologies
ofhousehold finance described in your book.
2. No designated ernployee of a state agency, including the Department of Health
and Human Services, may accept an honorarium from any source which is reportable on
the employee's statement of economic interests. However, the Act provides that an
honorarium does not include earned income for personal services, which are customarily
provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, orprofession.
Moreover, the Commission's regulations provide that theprohibition on receiving
honorarium does not extend to payments given in consideration for the sale ofbooks,
plays, or screenplays. Therefore, the Act does not prohibit you, as a state employee
whose position is designated in your agency's conflict of interest code, from receiving
payments for the sale and promotion of yourbook.
However, any training on the methodologies described in your book, which you
may provide to others through seminars or workshops, would constitute a "speech given"
within the meaning of section 89501(a) of the Act. If these seminars or workshops
constitute the predominant activity of your business, any income you earn in connection
with those seminars or workshops (other than payments received in consideration of the
sale of your book at the evant) would be prohibited honorarium, as long as you remain a
state employee.
FACTS
You are employed by the California Department of Health Services ("DHS') as a
"Nurse Evaluator II" in the MediCal Operations Division. This is a position which is
designated in the DHS's conflict of interest code-a Generally, yourjob requires you to
audit medical records in order to evaluate and monitor:
o The appropriateness of admissions, lengths of stay, and referrals to the
MediCal Case Management Program;
o The level of care and length of stay of patients in the Long Term Care
Program/Intermediate Care F acilities for the developmentally disabled;
and
' Thes" prohibitions are more specifically described in the Commission Fact Sheet enclosed with
o A nurse evaluator is a position designated as either disclosure category 4 or category 5 in DHS's
this letter
conflict of interest code.


I
File No. I-04-053
Page No. 3
. To facilitate shortened inpatient stay, safely discharge, and optimize
continuity of post-discharge care for patients in acute facilities.
Prior to accepting state employment you authored a theory of financial activities
in the home which you put into a writing entitled, My Household Financing Theory,
which you hope to have published in book form. You have created a business to obtain
publication of, and market, this writing. You have created a business to develop this
book and to date have secured a United States copyright registration number for your
writing and a business license from the City of Moreno Valley where you reside. You
have also contacted a software publisher regarding possible publicafion of your book. It
is possible that you may terminate your state employment at some point in order to work
solely on marketing your book, although you have no plans at presart to leave state
service.
ANALYSIS
1. Post-Employment Provisions
State administrative officialss who leave state service are subject to two types of
post-govemmental employment reskictions under the Act:
o A "one-yearban" prohibiting a state anployee from
communicating with his or her former agency to influence the agency's
administrative or legislative action6 (section 87406); and
o A "permanent ban" barring a state ernployee from "switching sides"
in any specific proceeding on which the employee worked while in state
service (sections 87 4OO-87 405).
You have not yet decided whether you will leave your state employment in order
to seek publication of, and market, your book. Nor have you provided specific facts or
framed particular questions which would allow us to advise you on how the post-
employment provisions of the Act are to be applied in the circumstances you will face in
connection with these efforts. Rather, you are seeking general information which would
5 A "state administrative official" is defined in section 87400(b) as "every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities
engages in anyjudicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or
ministerial capacitSr. "
' 
6 "Influencing legislative or administrative action" includes influencing "by any means, including
but not limited to the provision or use of information, statistics, studies or analyses-" (Section 82032.)
"Administrative action" is defined in section 82002 as "the proposal, drafting, development, consideration,
amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation, or other action in any
ratemaking proceeding or any quasiJegislative proceeding . . . ." Section 82037 defines "legislative
action" as "the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, enactment or defeat of any bill,
resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or any
committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature
acting in his official capacity. 'kgislative action' also means the action of the Govemor in approving or
vetoing any bill."


File No. I-04-053
Page No. 4
allow you to apply these provisions in the context of specific circumstances you might
face while seeking publication of, and marketing your book.
Accordingly, we are enclosing a copy of the Commission fact sheet, "Leaving
Your State Job? Post-Employment Restrictions May Affect You" to assist you in this
regard. You may also find helpful the Bresnan Advice Letter, No. A-03-269 and Boom
Advice Letter, No. I-03-157 (copies enclosed) in which advice was given to former state
administrative agency employees on how the post-govemmental employment provisions
of the Act are to be applied in the context of consulting and other services provided
through their wholly-owned businesses. Should you have specific questions in the
future, you may wish to seek our further assistance.
2. Prohibition on Receipt of Honorarium
An employee designatd under his or her agency's conflict of interest code is
prohibited from receiving an honorarium from any source if the honorarium is required to
be reported on his or her statement of economic interests. (Section 89502(c).) Section
89502(c) provides:
"No member of a state board or commission and no designated employee
of a state or local government agency shall accept an honorarium from any
source if the member or ernployee would be required to report the receipt
of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic
interests."
As a Nurse Evaluator II, you are a designated employee of DHS. (Section
82019.) Your job position is listed as either disclosure category 47 or disclosure category
58 in DHS's conflict of interest code. For this reason, if your business is a source of
income of $500 or more to you over a calendar year and your business either contracts
with, or receives grants or other monies from, the MediCal Operations Division of DHS
(disclosure category 4) or is regulated by or provides consultant services to the MediCal
Operations Division (disclosure calegory 5), you would be required to report that income
and its source. Potentially, therefore, this income could be subject to the honorarium ban
of section 89502(c), above. However, not all potential income, whether reportable or not,
constitutes "honorarium" subject to the ban on receipt of honorarium.
Section 89501 defines the term "honorarium." Section 89501 states in relevant
part:
"(a) For purposes of this chapter, 'honorarium' means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), any payment made in consideration for any speech grvfl,
7 This category reports investments, business positions, and sources of income in business entities
which conkact with, or receive grants or other monies from or through, your division.
8 This category reports investments, business positions andlources of income in business entities
which are licensed by, permitted by, have certificates from, or are otherwise regulated by orprovide
consultant services to, your division.


I
File No. I-04-053
Page No. 5
article published, or attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering."
Book Sales
Regulation 18931.2 states in relevant part:
"(a) Forputposes of Government Code Sections 89501 through 89506, an
'article published' means a nonfictional written work:
(1) That is produced in connection with any actiyity
other than the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession;
1t...fl
(c) 'Article' does not include books, plays or screenplays."
Thus, while you are a state employee, income you would receive from the sale of
your book would not constifute honorarium, since it would not be payment made in
consideration of an "article published."
Trainins Seminars and Workshops
Honorarium also does not include income from personal services provided as part
of a bona fide business, trade, or profession, unless speechmaking is a predominant
activity of that business, trade, or profession. In this regard, section 89501(b): provides:
"O) The term 'honorarium' does not include:
(l) Eamed income for personal services which are
customarilyprovided in connection with the practice of a
bona fide business, trade, or profession, such as teaching,
practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
building contracting, unless the sole or predominant
activity of the business, trade, or profession is making
speeches. The Commission shall adopt regulations to
implement this subdivision."
What constitutes a "bona fide profession" is not specifically defined in the statute.
However, regulation 18932.2 defines "teaching as a bona fide profession" for purposes of
the gift and honoraria provisions. Teaching only constitutes a bona fide profession when:
"(a) The individual is under contract or ernployed to teach at a
school, college, or university accredited approved or authorized as an
educational institution by an agency of the State of California; or by an
agency of any other state in the United States; or by an agency of the
and


File No. I-04-053
Page No. 6
Federal govemment; or by a bona fide independent accrediting
organization.
O) The individual receives payment for teaching a course,
presented to assist in maintenance or improvement ofprofessional skills or
knowledge where the course provides credit toward continuing education
requirements of the pertinent profession.
(c) The individual receives payment for teaching students enrolled
in an examination preparation program, such as a State Bar examination
review course-"
(Regul atio n I 8932.2(a)-(c). )
Thus, should you provide serninars or workshops to train others in the
methodologies described in your book and receive payment in connection with providing
those seminars, your actions in providing this haining would not constifute "teaching."
(Conover Advice Letter,No. A-02-2A7,copy enclosed.) Accordingly, thepayments you
would receive for these actions would not qualifu under the teaching exception of the
honorarium ban. Instead, as a "public address, oration, or other form oforal
presentation" whether glven individually or as a participant "in a panel, seminar, or
debate" these training seminars or workshops would constitute a "qreech glven" within
the meaning of section 89501(a) and regulation 18931 .1.
Two remaining factors contribute with the above when determining whether any
payments received in connection with your conducting seminars or workshops to train
others in the methodologies described in your book constitute prohibited honorarium.
First, are the seminars or workshops provided in connection with a business, kade or
profession? Second, if so, is providing the seminars or workshops the sole or
predominant activity of the business, trade, or profession? (Section 89501(b) and
regulations 18932,18932.1 arrd 18932.1.)e (Fulton Advice Letter, No. A-03-278, copy
enclosed.)
Regulation 18932.1(a) - (c) explains that a business, trade, or profession is
presumed to be "bona fide" when certain recordkeeping criteria are met, professional
licensure is held, or when an individual is ernployed by a college or university. For a
business in existence for less than two calendar years, a business is presumed to be bona
fide if:
"(l) Its owner has maintained records of income and expenses,
consistent with the operation of a business, from the date of inception of
the claimed business; and,
e As set forth in section 89501(bxl) income earned for providing personal services which are
"customarily provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade or profession" is
specifically exempted from the scope of the honoraria prohibitions, provided the sole orpredominant
activity ofthe business, trade, or profession is not making speeches.


,
File No. I-04-053
Page No. 7
(2) Any three of the following are maintained:
(A) copies of tax returns filed in connection with the operation of the
claimed business for one year;
(B) Records of bank accounts or lines of credit in the name of the
business;
(C) Records ofpurchase,lease, orrental of equipment and supplies for
the use solely by the business;
(D) Records of expandifures for rent, wages, business or professional
insurance, or other similar, customary costs of doing business;
(E) Records of efforts to market goods or services provided by the
business; or
(F) Documentation of expertise by the business operator in connection
with the service provided or product sold by the business."
(Regulation I 8932. I (bXl X2).)
Assuming that these recordkeeping requireme'nts are met, it still must be determined that
speechmaking is not the sole or predominant activity of the business. For a business in
existence less than one year, speechmaking is the sole orpredominant activity of the
business if:
"(1) During the 30-day period prior to and including the date of the
speech, more than 50 percent of the hours spent on an individual's
business, trade, or profession is devoted to the preparation and/or delivery
ofspeeches; or
(2) During the 30-day period prior to and including the date of the
speech, more than 50 percent of the gross income of an individual's
business, trade, or profession is derived from the preparation and/or
delivery of speeches."
(Regulation I 8932.3(bXl ) and (2).)
If these criteria are met with respect to the actions you undertake in order to train
others in the methodologies described in your book, then "making speeches" would be
the predominant activity of your business. I{, at that time, you are a state employee, the
income you would earn through your business in providing these seminars or workshops
would constitute honorarium, prohibited under section 89502(c).
Potential Conflicts of Interest
Please note that a public official may not'tnake," "participate in making" or
"influence" a governmental decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his or her economic interests, including sources of income. (Sections
87100 and 87103.)10 consequently, while you remain an-employee of the DHS, you
'o The Commission document, "Can I Vote? Overview of the Conflicts Laws" explains the steps
of this analysis and is enclosed for your information.


File No. I-04-053
Page No. 8
would have a conflict of interest prohibiting you from making, participating in making, or
influencing, a decision of that agency which could reasonably be foreseen as having a
material financial effect upon any source of income to the wholly-owned business which
you formed to promote your book. You may wish to request specific advice from the
Commission regarding your responsibilities under the Act should you, as an ernployee of
DHS, be confronted at a future date with potential involvement in a decision, which may
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon an individual or entity which
is a source of income to you or your business-
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
By:
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
KLGjg

(B) Records of bank accounts or lines of credit in the name of the
business;
(C) Records ofpurchase,lease, orrental of equipment and supplies for
the use solely by the business;
(D) Records of expandifures for rent, wages, business or professional
insurance, or other similar, customary costs of doing business;
(E) Records of efforts to market goods or services provided by the
business; or
(F) Documentation of expertise by the business operator in connection
with the service provided or product sold by the business."
(Regulation I 8932. I (bXl X2).)
Assuming that these recordkeeping requireme'nts are met, it still must be determined that
speechmaking is not the sole or predominant activity of the business. For a business in
existence less than one year, speechmaking is the sole orpredominant activity of the
business if:
"(1) During the 30-day period prior to and including the date of the
speech, more than 50 percent of the hours spent on an individual's
business, trade, or profession is devoted to the preparation and/or delivery
ofspeeches; or
(2) During the 30-day period prior to and including the date of the
speech, more than 50 percent of the gross income of an individual's
business, trade, or profession is derived from the preparation and/or
delivery of speeches."
(Regulation I 8932.3(bXl ) and (2).)
If these criteria are met with respect to the actions you undertake in order to train
others in the methodologies described in your book, then "making speeches" would be
the predominant activity of your business. I{, at that time, you are a state employee, the
income you would earn through your business in providing these seminars or workshops
would constitute honorarium, prohibited under section 89502(c).
Potential Conflicts of Interest
Please note that a public official may not'tnake," "participate in making" or
"influence" a governmental decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his or her economic interests, including sources of income. (Sections
87100 and 87103.)10 consequently, while you remain an-employee of the DHS, you
'o The Commission document, "Can I Vote? Overview of the Conflicts Laws" explains the steps
of this analysis and is enclosed for your information.
would have a conflict of interest prohibiting you from making, participating in making, or
influencing, a decision of that agency which could reasonably be foreseen as having a
material financial effect upon any source of income to the wholly-owned business which
you formed to promote your book. You may wish to request specific advice from the
Commission regarding your responsibilities under the Act should you, as an ernployee of
DHS, be confronted at a future date with potential involvement in a decision, which may
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon an individual or entity which
is a source of income to you or your business-

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Should you leave state service, would thepost-governmental employment
provisions of the Act restrict your activities in connection with the future publication and
promotion of a book you authored?
2. As a state employee, do the "honorarium" provisions of the Act prohibit you
from receiving payment in connection with the sale of your book, or seminars or
workshops you provide to train others in the methodologies described in your book?

CONCLUSION: 1. The post-governmental ernployment provisions of the Act consist of two
prohibitions: first, a "one-year ban" prohibiting a former state anployee from
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
:


I
communicating with or appearing before his or her former agency to influence the
agency's administrative or legislative action; and second, a "permanent ban" barring a
state employee from "switching sides" in any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
on which the employee worked while in state service.3 Thus, should you leave state
service, the post-employment provisions of the Act would not apply to the sale and
promotion of your book, or the training you may provide to others in the methodologies
ofhousehold finance described in your book.
2. No designated ernployee of a state agency, including the Department of Health
and Human Services, may accept an honorarium from any source which is reportable on
the employee's statement of economic interests. However, the Act provides that an
honorarium does not include earned income for personal services, which are customarily
provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, orprofession.
Moreover, the Commission's regulations provide that theprohibition on receiving
honorarium does not extend to payments given in consideration for the sale ofbooks,
plays, or screenplays. Therefore, the Act does not prohibit you, as a state employee
whose position is designated in your agency's conflict of interest code, from receiving
payments for the sale and promotion of yourbook.
However, any training on the methodologies described in your book, which you
may provide to others through seminars or workshops, would constitute a "speech given"
within the meaning of section 89501(a) of the Act. If these seminars or workshops
constitute the predominant activity of your business, any income you earn in connection
with those seminars or workshops (other than payments received in consideration of the
sale of your book at the evant) would be prohibited honorarium, as long as you remain a
state employee.
